chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: Docker, a tool that can package software into containers that run reliably in any environment.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: But what is a container and why do you need one?

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: Let's imagine you built an app with COBOL that runs on some weird flavor of Linux.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: You want to share this app with your friend, but he has an entirely different system.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: So the problem becomes, how do we replicate the environment our software needs on any machine?

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: One way to package an app is with a virtual machine, where the hardware is simulated,

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: then installed with the required OS and dependencies.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: This allows us to run multiple apps on the same infrastructure.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: However, because each VM is running its own operating system, they tend to be bulky and slow.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: Now, a Docker container is conceptually very similar to a VM with one key difference.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: Instead of virtualizing hardware, containers only virtualize the OS.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: Or in other words, all apps or containers are run by a single kernel,

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: and this makes almost everything faster and more efficient.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: There are three fundamental elements in the universe of Docker.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: The Dockerfile, the image, and the container.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: The Dockerfile is like DNA.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: It's just code that tells Docker how to build an image,

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: which itself is a snapshot of your software,

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: along with all of its dependencies down to the operating system level.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: The image is immutable, and it can be used to spin up multiple containers,

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: which is your actual software running in the real world.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: Create a Dockerfile and use from to start from an existing template like Ubuntu.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: This base image gets pulled down from the cloud,

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: and you can also upload your own images to a variety of different Docker registries.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: From there, you might want to use run to run a terminal command

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: that installs dependencies into your image.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: You can set environment variables and do all kinds of other stuff.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: Then the last thing you'll do is set a default command that's executed

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: when you start up a container.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: And now we can create the image file by running the Docker build command.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: It goes through each step in our Dockerfile to build the image layer by layer.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: We can then bring this image to life as a container with the Docker run command.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: As your app demands more resources, you can run it on multiple machines,

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: multiple clouds, on-prem, or wherever you want reliably.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: This has been Docker in 100 seconds.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: If you enjoyed it, make sure to like and subscribe,

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: and stay tuned for more Docker content coming to this channel soon.

chunks.title: Docker
chunks.text: Thanks for watching, and I will see you in the next one.

text: Docker, a tool that can package software into containers that run reliably in any environment. But what is a container and why do you need one? Let's imagine you built an app with COBOL that runs on some weird flavor of Linux. You want to share this app with your friend, but he has an entirely different system. So the problem becomes, how do we replicate the environment our software needs on any machine? One way to package an app is with a virtual machine, where the hardware is simulated, then installed with the required OS and dependencies. This allows us to run multiple apps on the same infrastructure. However, because each VM is running its own operating system, they tend to be bulky and slow. Now, a Docker container is conceptually very similar to a VM with one key difference. Instead of virtualizing hardware, containers only virtualize the OS. Or in other words, all apps or containers are run by a single kernel, and this makes almost everything faster and more efficient. There are three fundamental elements in the universe of Docker. The Dockerfile, the image, and the container. The Dockerfile is like DNA. It's just code that tells Docker how to build an image, which itself is a snapshot of your software, along with all of its dependencies down to the operating system level. The image is immutable, and it can be used to spin up multiple containers, which is your actual software running in the real world. Create a Dockerfile and use from to start from an existing template like Ubuntu. This base image gets pulled down from the cloud, and you can also upload your own images to a variety of different Docker registries. From there, you might want to use run to run a terminal command that installs dependencies into your image. You can set environment variables and do all kinds of other stuff. Then the last thing you'll do is set a default command that's executed when you start up a container. And now we can create the image file by running the Docker build command. It goes through each step in our Dockerfile to build the image layer by layer. We can then bring this image to life as a container with the Docker run command. As your app demands more resources, you can run it on multiple machines, multiple clouds, on-prem, or wherever you want reliably. This has been Docker in 100 seconds. If you enjoyed it, make sure to like and subscribe, and stay tuned for more Docker content coming to this channel soon. Thanks for watching, and I will see you in the next one.
